title: Royal anthem

infobox.value: Office/rank | Music | Notes

[President](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina") | "[Marcha de Ituzaingó](/w/index.php?title=Ituzaing%C3%B3_March&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ituzaingó March (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcha_de_Ituzaing%C3%B3 "es:Marcha de Ituzaingó")]" | Presidential March[[4]](#cite_note-4)
[Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") | "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[5]](#cite_note-ozgov-5) |

infobox.key: [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"), [Governors](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states")
infobox.value: *Vice-regal salute*[[5]](#cite_note-ozgov-5) | The first and last four bars of "[Advance Australia Fair](/wiki/Advance_Australia_Fair "Advance Australia Fair")", the national anthem.[[5]](#cite_note-ozgov-5) Before 1984, the royal salute was the first six bars of [God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King").

[President](/wiki/President_of_Brazil "President of Brazil") | "Continências ao Presidente da República" | Introduction and final chords of the [Brazilian National Anthem](/wiki/Brazilian_National_Anthem "Brazilian National Anthem"). Only applicable at military ceremonies.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), [consort](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_monarchs#Consorts "List of Canadian monarchs") | "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[7]](#cite_note-pch-7)
The first six bars of "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[7]](#cite_note-pch-7)

"Salute to the Governor General/Lieutenant Governor", commonly called the *Vice Regal Salute* | The first six bars of "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")" immediately followed by the first four and last four bars of "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")", the national anthem. For a [pipe band](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band"), a combination of "Mallorca" and "O Canada" is played instead.[[7]](#cite_note-pch-7)

[President](/wiki/President_of_the_Czech_Republic "President of the Czech Republic") | "Fanfáry z Libuše" | Fanfares from overture of the opera [*Libuše*](/wiki/Libu%C5%A1e_(opera) "Libuše (opera)").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Denmark "Monarchy of Denmark") | "[Kong Christian stod ved højen mast](/wiki/Kong_Christian_stod_ved_h%C3%B8jen_mast "Kong Christian stod ved højen mast")" | Also one of two national anthems, the other being "[Der er et yndigt land](/wiki/Der_er_et_yndigt_land "Der er et yndigt land")".[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[President](/wiki/President_of_Ecuador "President of Ecuador") | "Honores al Presidente de la Republica" (Presidential Salute) "Marcha Presidential"  (*Presidential March*) | 1. In the [Army](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Army "Ecuadorian Army"), [Air Force](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Air_Force "Ecuadorian Air Force") and Police, a bugle fanfare followed by the chorus of "[Salve, Oh Patria](/wiki/Salve,_Oh_Patria "Salve, Oh Patria")"[[10]](#cite_note-10) 2. In the [Navy](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Navy "Ecuadorian Navy"), four long blasts of the [boatswain's call](/wiki/Boatswain%27s_call "Boatswain's call") then the rest as above 3. Played at the arrival of the president in military events, once the arrival honors have been received.

[President](/wiki/President_of_Estonia "President of Estonia") | "[Pidulik marss](/wiki/Pidulik_marss "Pidulik marss")" ("Solemn March", or Presidential March) | In 1922, "Pidulik marss" won the contest for Estonian-composed state march. On 27 January 1923, it was adopted by the then-[Minister of War](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(Estonia) "Minister of Defence (Estonia)") [Jaan Soots](/wiki/Jaan_Soots "Jaan Soots") as the honorary march of the State Elder of Estonia (later known as the president of the Republic of Estonia), thus replacing the "Porilaste marss" which was previously used in its place.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

infobox.key: [Commander of the Estonian Defence Forces](/wiki/Commander_of_the_Estonian_Defence_Forces "Commander of the Estonian Defence Forces") and Commander-in-Chief of the [Estonian Defence Forces](/wiki/Estonian_Defence_Forces "Estonian Defence Forces")
infobox.value: "[Porilaste marss](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rneborgarnas_marsch "Björneborgarnas marsch")" was the honorary march of the State Elder of Estonia until 27 January 1923 when it was replaced by the "Pidulik marss". | "March of the [Pori Regiment](/wiki/Pori_Regiment "Pori Regiment")"/"March of the Björneborgers". The tune was first publicly performed in Estonia at the 7th [Estonian Song Festival](/wiki/Estonian_Song_Festival "Estonian Song Festival") in 1910, a handful of years before the declaration of independence.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[President](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") | "[Chant du départ](/wiki/Chant_du_d%C3%A9part "Chant du départ")" | Adopted by then-president [Valéry Giscard d'Estaing](/wiki/Val%C3%A9ry_Giscard_d%27Estaing "Valéry Giscard d'Estaing") in 1974 as the personal anthem of the president of France.[[15]](#cite_note-napoleon-15)

[President](/wiki/President_of_Haiti "President of Haiti") | "[Quand nos Aïeux brisèrent leurs entraves](/wiki/Quand_nos_A%C3%AFeux_bris%C3%A8rent_leurs_entraves "Quand nos Aïeux brisèrent leurs entraves")" (Chant Nationale) | "When Our Fathers Broke Their Chains" (also known as the National Hymn). Poem by [Oswald Durand](/wiki/Oswald_Durand "Oswald Durand"), set to music by [Occide Jeanty](/wiki/Occide_Jeanty "Occide Jeanty") in 1893 to serve as a national anthem; replaced by "[La Dessalinienne](/wiki/La_Dessalinienne "La Dessalinienne")" in 1904.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[President](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland") | "Presidential Salute" | The first four and last five bars of "[Amhrán na bhFiann](/wiki/Amhr%C3%A1n_na_bhFiann "Amhrán na bhFiann")", the national anthem.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

"Mór Chluana" / "Amhrán Dóchais" | "Mór Chluana" ("More of [Cloyne](/wiki/Cloyne "Cloyne")") is a traditional air collected by [Patrick Weston Joyce](/wiki/Patrick_Weston_Joyce "Patrick Weston Joyce") in 1873.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-ainm-19) "Amhrán Dóchais" ("Song of Hope") is a poem written by [Osborn Bergin](/wiki/Osborn_Bergin "Osborn Bergin") in 1913 and set to the air.[[19]](#cite_note-ainm-19)[[20]](#cite_note-cuiv-20) [John A. Costello](/wiki/John_A._Costello "John A. Costello") chose the air as his salute.[[20]](#cite_note-cuiv-20) Though the salute is often called "Amhrán Dóchais", [Brian Ó Cuív](/wiki/Brian_%C3%93_Cu%C3%ADv "Brian Ó Cuív") argues "Mór Chluana" is the correct title.[[20]](#cite_note-cuiv-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[President](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") | "[S'hymnu sardu nationale](/wiki/S%27hymnu_sardu_nationale "S'hymnu sardu nationale")" ("Inno Sardo Nazionale") | First adopted in 1991.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[President](/wiki/President_of_South_Korea "President of South Korea") | "Phoenix Hymn" | Modified version of Star March, played during military parades or events attended by the President.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[Raja Permaisuri Agong](/wiki/Raja_Permaisuri_Agong "Raja Permaisuri Agong"); [Yang di-Pertua Negeri](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertua_Negeri "Yang di-Pertua Negeri") | Abridged version of [the National Anthem](/wiki/Negaraku "Negaraku") | Consisting of first and last sections.[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2) Played before the relevant [state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia")'s anthem[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2) if the salute is for the Yang di-Pertua Negeri.

Short version of "[Negaraku](/wiki/Negaraku "Negaraku")" (the national anthem) | Consisting of last section. Played after the relevant [state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia")'s anthem. Only may be played if the state monarch is present representing the [King](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia").[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2)

Members of the [Royal House](/wiki/Dutch_Royal_House "Dutch Royal House"); [Governor of Aruba](/wiki/Governor_of_Aruba "Governor of Aruba"); [Governor of Curaçao](/wiki/Governor_of_Cura%C3%A7ao "Governor of Curaçao") and [Governor of Sint Maarten](/wiki/Governor_of_Sint_Maarten "Governor of Sint Maarten") | "[Het Wilhelmus](/wiki/Het_Wilhelmus "Het Wilhelmus")" | The national anthem.[[3]](#cite_note-mindefnl-3)

in several [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") for the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General "Governor-General"), Governor, or Lieutenant Governor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Ruffles and flourishes](/wiki/Ruffles_and_flourishes "Ruffles and flourishes") may be played instead of, or preceding, such an anthem.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Royal anthem](/w/index.php?title=Royal_anthem&redirect=no "Royal anthem"))

Ceremonial music for a particular office

The [191st Army Band](/wiki/191st_Army_Band "191st Army Band") performs three [Ruffles and Flourishes](/wiki/Ruffles_and_Flourishes "Ruffles and Flourishes") which is followed by the "General's March" as honors are rendered to General [Robert W. Cone](/wiki/Robert_W._Cone "Robert W. Cone") at [Fort Hood](/wiki/Fort_Hood "Fort Hood").

The anthem for a person, office or rank is music played on formal or ceremonial occasions in the presence of the person, office-holder, or rank-holder, especially by a [military band](/wiki/Military_band "Military band").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") in many countries is honored with a prescribed piece of music; in some countries the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") serves this purpose, while others have a separate **royal**, **presidential**, or, historically, **imperial anthem**.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other officials may also have anthems, such as the **vice-regal salute** in several [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") for the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General "Governor-General"), Governor, or Lieutenant Governor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Ruffles and flourishes](/wiki/Ruffles_and_flourishes "Ruffles and flourishes") may be played instead of, or preceding, such an anthem.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_anthem&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Examples")]

Countries where the national anthem is also the head of state's personal anthem include [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2) the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"),[[3]](#cite_note-mindefnl-3)

Other examples include the following:

| [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Argentina "President of Argentina") | "[Marcha de Ituzaingó](/w/index.php?title=Ituzaing%C3%B3_March&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ituzaingó March (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcha_de_Ituzaing%C3%B3 "es:Marcha de Ituzaingó")]" | Presidential March[[4]](#cite_note-4) |
| [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") | [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") | "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[5]](#cite_note-ozgov-5) |  |

Only applicable at military ceremonies.[[6]](#cite_note-6) |

| Other [Royal Family](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada#Canada's_royal_family_and_house "Monarchy of Canada") members | The first six bars of "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[7]](#cite_note-pch-7) |

For a [pipe band](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band"), a combination of "Mallorca" and "O Canada" is played instead.[[7]](#cite_note-pch-7) |

| [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Ecuador "President of Ecuador") | "Honores al Presidente de la Republica" (Presidential Salute) "Marcha Presidential"  (*Presidential March*) | 1. In the [Army](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Army "Ecuadorian Army"), [Air Force](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Air_Force "Ecuadorian Air Force") and Police, a bugle fanfare followed by the chorus of "[Salve, Oh Patria](/wiki/Salve,_Oh_Patria "Salve, Oh Patria")"[[10]](#cite_note-10) 2. In the [Navy](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Navy "Ecuadorian Navy"), four long blasts of the [boatswain's call](/wiki/Boatswain%27s_call "Boatswain's call") then the rest as above 3. Played at the arrival of the president in military events, once the arrival honors have been received. |
| [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Estonia "President of Estonia") | "[Pidulik marss](/wiki/Pidulik_marss "Pidulik marss")" ("Solemn March", or Presidential March) | In 1922, "Pidulik marss" won the contest for Estonian-composed state march. On 27 January 1923, it was adopted by the then-[Minister of War](/wiki/Minister_of_Defence_(Estonia) "Minister of Defence (Estonia)") [Jaan Soots](/wiki/Jaan_Soots "Jaan Soots") as the honorary march of the State Elder of Estonia (later known as the president of the Republic of Estonia), thus replacing the "Porilaste marss" which was previously used in its place.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) |
| [Commander of the Estonian Defence Forces](/wiki/Commander_of_the_Estonian_Defence_Forces "Commander of the Estonian Defence Forces") and Commander-in-Chief of the [Estonian Defence Forces](/wiki/Estonian_Defence_Forces "Estonian Defence Forces") | "[Porilaste marss](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rneborgarnas_marsch "Björneborgarnas marsch")" was the honorary march of the State Elder of Estonia until 27 January 1923 when it was replaced by the "Pidulik marss". | "March of the [Pori Regiment](/wiki/Pori_Regiment "Pori Regiment")"/"March of the Björneborgers". The tune was first publicly performed in Estonia at the 7th [Estonian Song Festival](/wiki/Estonian_Song_Festival "Estonian Song Festival") in 1910, a handful of years before the declaration of independence.[[14]](#cite_note-14) |
| [France](/wiki/France "France") | [President](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") | "[Chant du départ](/wiki/Chant_du_d%C3%A9part "Chant du départ")" | Adopted by then-president [Valéry Giscard d'Estaing](/wiki/Val%C3%A9ry_Giscard_d%27Estaing "Valéry Giscard d'Estaing") in 1974 as the personal anthem of the president of France.[[15]](#cite_note-napoleon-15) |
| [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Haiti "President of Haiti") | "[Quand nos Aïeux brisèrent leurs entraves](/wiki/Quand_nos_A%C3%AFeux_bris%C3%A8rent_leurs_entraves "Quand nos Aïeux brisèrent leurs entraves")" (Chant Nationale) | "When Our Fathers Broke Their Chains" (also known as the National Hymn). Poem by [Oswald Durand](/wiki/Oswald_Durand "Oswald Durand"), set to music by [Occide Jeanty](/wiki/Occide_Jeanty "Occide Jeanty") in 1893 to serve as a national anthem; replaced by "[La Dessalinienne](/wiki/La_Dessalinienne "La Dessalinienne")" in 1904.[[16]](#cite_note-16) |
| [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland") | "Presidential Salute" | The first four and last five bars of "[Amhrán na bhFiann](/wiki/Amhr%C3%A1n_na_bhFiann "Amhrán na bhFiann")", the national anthem.[[17]](#cite_note-17) |
| [Taoiseach](/wiki/Taoiseach "Taoiseach") | "Mór Chluana" / "Amhrán Dóchais" | "Mór Chluana" ("More of [Cloyne](/wiki/Cloyne "Cloyne")") is a traditional air collected by [Patrick Weston Joyce](/wiki/Patrick_Weston_Joyce "Patrick Weston Joyce") in 1873.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-ainm-19) "Amhrán Dóchais" ("Song of Hope") is a poem written by [Osborn Bergin](/wiki/Osborn_Bergin "Osborn Bergin") in 1913 and set to the air.[[19]](#cite_note-ainm-19)[[20]](#cite_note-cuiv-20) [John A. Costello](/wiki/John_A._Costello "John A. Costello") chose the air as his salute.[[20]](#cite_note-cuiv-20) Though the salute is often called "Amhrán Dóchais", [Brian Ó Cuív](/wiki/Brian_%C3%93_Cu%C3%ADv "Brian Ó Cuív") argues "Mór Chluana" is the correct title.[[20]](#cite_note-cuiv-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) |
| [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") | "[S'hymnu sardu nationale](/wiki/S%27hymnu_sardu_nationale "S'hymnu sardu nationale")" ("Inno Sardo Nazionale") | First adopted in 1991.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) |
| [Korea (Republic of)](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") | [President](/wiki/President_of_South_Korea "President of South Korea") | "Phoenix Hymn" | Modified version of Star March, played during military parades or events attended by the President.[[24]](#cite_note-24) |
| [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") | [Raja Permaisuri Agong](/wiki/Raja_Permaisuri_Agong "Raja Permaisuri Agong"); [Yang di-Pertua Negeri](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertua_Negeri "Yang di-Pertua Negeri") | Abridged version of [the National Anthem](/wiki/Negaraku "Negaraku") | Consisting of first and last sections.[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2) Played before the relevant [state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia")'s anthem[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2) if the salute is for the Yang di-Pertua Negeri. |
| [State monarchs](/wiki/Monarchies_in_Malaysia "Monarchies in Malaysia") | Short version of "[Negaraku](/wiki/Negaraku "Negaraku")" (the national anthem) | Consisting of last section. Played after the relevant [state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia")'s anthem. Only may be played if the state monarch is present representing the [King](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia").[[2]](#cite_note-malaysia1968-2) |
| [Netherlands](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "Kingdom of the Netherlands") | Members of the [Royal House](/wiki/Dutch_Royal_House "Dutch Royal House"); [Governor of Aruba](/wiki/Governor_of_Aruba "Governor of Aruba"); [Governor of Curaçao](/wiki/Governor_of_Cura%C3%A7ao "Governor of Curaçao") and [Governor of Sint Maarten](/wiki/Governor_of_Sint_Maarten "Governor of Sint Maarten") | "[Het Wilhelmus](/wiki/Het_Wilhelmus "Het Wilhelmus")" | The national anthem.[[3]](#cite_note-mindefnl-3) |
| Various officials not entitled to "Het Wilhelmus"[[fn 1]](#cite_note-25) | "De Jonge Prins van Friesland"[[3]](#cite_note-mindefnl-3) | Ministers used the national anthem until [Queen Beatrix](/wiki/Beatrix_of_the_Netherlands "Beatrix of the Netherlands") objected in 1986.[[25]](#cite_note-26) |
| [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") | [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand "Monarchy of New Zealand") | "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[26]](#cite_note-nzgstq-27) | Also one of two national anthems, the other being "[God Defend New Zealand](/wiki/God_Defend_New_Zealand "God Defend New Zealand")".[[26]](#cite_note-nzgstq-27) |
| [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand") | "Salute to the Governor-General" | The first six bars of "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")".[[27]](#cite_note-28) The anthem may also be played in full.[[26]](#cite_note-nzgstq-27) |
| [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea") | [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Papua_New_Guinea "Monarchy of Papua New Guinea") | "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")"[[28]](#cite_note-29) |  |
| [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") | [President](/wiki/President_of_the_Philippines "President of the Philippines") | "[We Say Mabuhay](/wiki/We_Say_Mabuhay "We Say Mabuhay")"[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-Walsh2013-31) | The word [*mabuhay*](/wiki/Mabuhay_(expression) "Mabuhay (expression)") means "long live". The song, with music by [Tirso Cruz, Sr](/w/index.php?title=Tirso_Cruz,_Sr&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tirso Cruz, Sr (page does not exist)") and English lyrics by American James King Steele, was written c. 1935–40.[[30]](#cite_note-Walsh2013-31) Played to announce the arrival of the President during major events, minus the four ruffles and flourishes. |
| [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Poland "President of Poland") | "Sygnał prezydencki"[[31]](#cite_note-32) | Played in the presence of the President when the Presidential Ensign is raised in major events. |
| [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Romania "President of Romania") | "[Marș triumfal](/wiki/Mar%C8%99_triumfal "Marș triumfal")"[[32]](#cite_note-33) | Played in the presence of the president in state events and during state visits by foreign high officials. |
| [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") | [President](/wiki/President_of_Singapore "President of Singapore") | Abridged version of "[Majulah Singapura](/wiki/Majulah_Singapura "Majulah Singapura")" | First six bars only, played during state visits by foreign heads of state.[[33]](#cite_note-34) During major national events, the anthem is played in full. |
| [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") | [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Thailand "Monarchy of Thailand") | "[Sansoen Phra Barami](/wiki/Sansoen_Phra_Barami "Sansoen Phra Barami")"[[34]](#cite_note-35) | "The song of glorifying His Majesty's prestige". Former national anthem, still played before shows in cinemas and theatres and during all major events when the King and Queen are present. |
| "Sadudee Jom Racha" | "Hymn to the Righteous King". Used for ceremonies related to King [Vajiralongkorn](/wiki/Vajiralongkorn "Vajiralongkorn") and Queen [Suthida](/wiki/Suthida "Suthida").[[35]](#cite_note-36) |
| Other members of the [royal family](/wiki/Thai_Royal_Family "Thai Royal Family") | "Maha Chai" | "Grand Victory". Also used for regents and military officers who held the rank of Field Marshal, Admiral of the Fleet and/or Marshal of the Royal Thai Air Force.[[36]](#cite_note-thai_salute-37) |
| Other situations | "Maha Roek" | "Grand Auspice". Mainly used for the arrival of senior government officials and for inaugurations. It is also used as the General Salute Music of the [Royal Thai Armed Forces](/wiki/Royal_Thai_Armed_Forces "Royal Thai Armed Forces").[[37]](#cite_note-38) |
| [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") | [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") | "[Hail to the Chief](/wiki/Hail_to_the_Chief "Hail to the Chief")"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) |
| [Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") | "[Hail Columbia](/wiki/Hail_Columbia "Hail Columbia")"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) | First 8 and last 4 bars |
| Various officials[[fn 2]](#cite_note-40) | "Honors March 1"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) | 32-bar medley of "[Stars and Stripes Forever](/wiki/Stars_and_Stripes_Forever "Stars and Stripes Forever")"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) |
| [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"), [Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"), and [Space Force](/wiki/United_States_Space_Force "United States Space Force") general officers | "General's March"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) | Also listed in AR 600-25 as "Honors March 2" |
| [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") flag officers | "Admiral's March"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) | Also listed in AR 600-25 as "Honors March 3" |
| [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") admirals and [Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") generals | "Flag Officer's March"[[38]](#cite_note-usmilregs-39) | Also listed in AR 600-25 as "Honors March 4" |
